tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

obj.description:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.text: Let me check what schemas are available to find one for organizations.
Let me first check the available schemas to find the right type for an organization.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTAF</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.organization</typename>
</object>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

content.text: Let me check what schemas are available to find one for organizations.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.

Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

  }

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, image, and location. |

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas. Let me list them.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas and their typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, image, and location. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with rich fields including name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, URLs, birthday, and more. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging other objects. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that can fire functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |

Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to work with any of these types!

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas and their typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, image, and location. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with rich fields including name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, URLs, birthday, and more. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging other objects. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that can fire functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |

Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to work with any of these types!

params.success:
# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-2,1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> <cite index="5-1">DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.</cite>

<cite index="9-3">DXOS — The decentralized operating system — is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="18-1">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="18-2">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Type:** <cite index="18-4">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="18-3">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Size:** <cite index="14-2">11-50 employees.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="18-6">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **Community:** <cite index="31-7">8 cities worldwide, 30+ contributors, 200+ community members.</cite>

## Mission & Vision

<cite index="2-1,2-4">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

<cite index="9-6,9-7">The DXOS platform consists of decentralized protocols, tools, and applications that provide an alternative to the current Web that has been overtaken by the Big Tech hegemony. DXOS enables the development of privacy-preserving applications for its users, and a fairer marketplace for open source developers.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack

DXOS is built around three core protocols/components:

### 1. ECHO (Data)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-11">ECHO serves as a database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="6-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Networking)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### Underlying CRDT Technology
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>

## Key Architecture Principles

- **Cloudless:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **Peer-to-Peer:** <cite index="1-17">User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.</cite>
- **WebRTC Sync:** <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- **Local-First:** <cite index="12-3,12-4">DXOS provides developers with a toolkit to build local-first, collaborative apps which preserve privacy by communicating peer-to-peer. DXOS applications work offline, share state instantly when online, and leave end-users in control of their data and privacy.</cite>
- **Signaling Servers:** <cite index="6-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>

## Developer Experience / SDK

<cite index="4-5,4-6">DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.</cite>

<cite index="3-4">The main repository contains the TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>

<cite index="33-5">The framework is open-source under the MIT license.</cite>

<cite index="4-27,4-28">DXOS recommends Vite as the bundler. Vite requires a plugin in order to serve the WebAssembly modules.</cite>

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="5-2">DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="36-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

<cite index="20-2">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.</cite>

### Composer Features:
- **Docs:** <cite index="31-17">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- **Comments:** <cite index="31-19">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- **Plugins:** <cite index="31-39,31-40">Everything inside of Composer is a plugin. This means you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need to take programmatic workflows to the next level.</cite>
- **Sheets:** <cite index="35-12,35-13">Supports 300+ basic calculations along with support for custom functions. Extendable via API calls and custom script integrations.</cite>
- **Sketch:** <cite index="37-2">The Sketch plugin allows you to leverage a digital whiteboard and diagram editor powered by TLDraw.</cite>
- **Scripts:** <cite index="37-7">Scripts in Composer allow you to deploy custom functions that run on the edge.</cite>
- **Meetings:** <cite index="37-4">Built-in voice/video meetings with real-time transcription, task extraction & semantic CRM enhancements.</cite>
- **AI Integration:** <cite index="31-29">Leverage machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting your intellectual property.</cite>
- **Data Ownership:** <cite index="31-1,31-2">You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>
- **Privacy:** <cite index="17-4,17-5">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite>
- **Import/Export:** <cite index="31-51">Import and export your entire database as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>

### Agents
<cite index="17-9,17-10">Agents are DXOS instances running without an attached user interface. A common use of agents is to run on a server and provide availability if all other devices are offline, but this is completely optional.</cite> <cite index="6-2">DXOS is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite>

## Organizational Structure

<cite index="9-4,9-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite>

<cite index="30-3,30-4">Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.</cite>

## Current Status

<cite index="7-7,7-8">The DXOS SDK is under active development, however the current focus is building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. To this end, they're currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK, and will prioritize suggestions and contributions that align with their work on Composer.</cite>

<cite index="36-2,36-3">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite>

## Key Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://www.dxos.org/composer

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

# DXOS (Decentralized Operating System)

## Overview
DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.
DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.

The company is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. It offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.

## Company Details

- **Founded:**
DXOS was founded in 2021.
- **Headquarters:**
DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.
- **Status:**
DXOS is a Private company.
- **Industry:**
DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.
- **Team:**
DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.
- **Investors:**
Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.
## Mission & Philosophy

DXOS is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.
Their mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK includes three main technology pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Database)
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.
It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.
### 2. HALO (Identity)
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.
HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.
### 3. MESH (Networking)
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.
## How It Works
DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.
User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.
It automatically synchronizes data across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.
There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.
## SDK & Developer Experience
DXOS wraps its three core components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.

DXOS is a decentralized operating system framework that empowers developers to build local-first, collaborative applications without relying on central servers. By providing a comprehensive SDK and toolchain, DXOS facilitates the creation of apps that prioritize user privacy, offline functionality, and seamless peer-to-peer synchronization.

The DXOS SDK is under active development, with a current focus on building features and functionality that enable the development of Composer. To this end, they're currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK.
## Flagship Application: Composer
DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.
Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.
Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.

Key principles of Composer:
- **Extensibility:**

Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic — anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.
- **Collaboration:**
Composer has real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model, which makes a lot of things better: from meeting notes to working on airplanes.
- **Privacy:**
Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.
- **Openness:**

Composer and the DXOS framework are open source. Due to the CRDT-based implementation there's no need for a server other than for backup. DXOS provides backup services if you want them, but you can also host your own.
Everything in the app is provided by plugins. This has been a helpful driver of clean separation of concerns.

### Composer Features
-
Real-time collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.
Collaborating with threaded comments across multiple document formats.
Building custom plugins to enhance functionality.
A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.
Building custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.
Importing and exporting entire databases as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.
## DXOS Agents

DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in. Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.

DXOS is an open platform with a focus on privacy. Anyone can run a regular peer on any machine they want and that would qualify as an agent. They also offer an open-source CLI tool which can run the agent on any machine from the command line.
DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.
## GitHub & Open Source
DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.
Their main repo is a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.

The project is licensed under MIT.

## Community
The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.
The project boasts contributors across 8 cities worldwide, 30+ contributors, and 200+ community members.

## Key Links
- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer

---

params.success:
## DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) — Research Summary

### Overview
DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers. It stands for "Decentralized Operating System" and is a platform for building fully decentralized applications.

The company is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. It offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications.

### Company Details
- **Founded:** 2021
- **Headquarters:** New York, NY
- **Status:** Private company
- **Industry:** Software Development Applications
- **Team:** Created by a small core team of developers from many countries
- **Investors:** Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States)
- **Community:** 8 cities worldwide, 30+ contributors, 200+ community members

### Mission & Philosophy
DXOS is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace enhanced by AI capabilities. Their mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.

### Core Technology Stack

**1. ECHO (Database)**
An open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage. It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.

**2. HALO (Identity)**
Seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs, allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices. It is a decentralized identity and access control protocol.

**3. MESH (Networking)**
Extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.

### How It Works
- Leverages **Automerge**, a powerful CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
- The only centralized server needed is to serve the application shell — everything else happens client-side and peer-to-peer.
- User identity, data storage, synchronization, and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.
- Uses **WebRTC** for cloudless peer-to-peer networking and automatic data synchronization across devices.
- Signaling servers are used for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer — no servers have access to the data.

### SDK & Developer Experience
- SDKs are available for React and TypeScript.
- Provides a comprehensive SDK and toolchain for creating apps that prioritize user privacy, offline functionality, and seamless peer-to-peer synchronization.
- The SDK is under active development, with current focus on enabling the development of Composer.
- Licensed under MIT.

### Flagship Application: Composer
Composer is a collaborative productivity app and extensible IDE, built for both real-time and async collaboration. It is an open source "Super App" that provides teams with a fully customizable and extensible environment.

**Key Composer Features:**
- Blends popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables)
- Real-time collaboration inside a modern markdown editor
- Threaded comments across multiple document formats
- Custom plugin support to enhance functionality
- Local-first spreadsheet with custom functions
- Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window
- Import/export databases as plain markdown, JSON, and media files
- Private AI processing against knowledge locally
- Custom schemas for structured data
- Extensibility at its core — everything is a plugin, built using the same App Framework APIs

**Core Principles:**
- **Extensibility:** Built for customization from the ground up
- **Collaboration:** Real-time multiplayer through local-first model
- **Privacy:** Private by default, data stored on your device, only shared with explicitly invited others
- **Openness:** Fully open source, CRDT-based, self-hostable

### DXOS Agents
Agents address a key challenge in peer-to-peer systems: when all peers go offline, new peers cannot replicate. Agents are just like any other peer but with more uptime. They typically run on servers or always-on desktop machines. DXOS offers an open-source CLI tool for running agents and is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents for those who don't want to run their own infrastructure.

### Key Links
- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos (64 repositories)
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer
